title: Remote Bindings: One Source of Truth for Local Dev
author: Patrouch & ZCode
excerpt: Why maintain GLB models, audio files, and database rows in two places? Cloudflare's per-binding remote flag lets local dev hit the real R2 and D1 directly — no duplication, no sync, no seed scripts.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: The munchkins are showing as capsules again. We uploaded the GLBs to R2, registered them in D1, and it worked. Then I restarted the dev server and they're capsules.

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content:
The library API is returning `{ models: [], error: 'D1_ERROR: no such table: asset_library' }`. The table exists on remote — I verified it with `wrangler d1 execute --remote`. The problem is that SvelteKit's dev server is hitting a **local** miniflare D1 instance, not the remote one.

The `platformProxy: { remote: true }` in the vite config looks like it should proxy to remote, but reading wrangler 4.112's source code reveals the truth: `remote` isn't the right option name. The actual check is `options.remoteBindings !== false`, and remote bindings are enabled by default at the wrangler level. The real switch is a **per-binding `"remote": true` flag** in wrangler.jsonc itself.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: So every D1 database and R2 bucket needs its own remote flag?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content:
Exactly. Each binding independently declares whether dev should route to the real Cloudflare resource. So in wrangler.jsonc:

```jsonc
"d1_databases": [{
  "binding": "DB_ASSETS",
  "database_name": "webxr-assets",
  "remote": true   // ← this is the switch
}],
"r2_buckets": [{
  "binding": "R2_ASSETS",
  "bucket_name": "webxr-assets",
  "remote": true
}]
```

Now dev hits the real remote D1 and R2 directly. No local copies of GLBs, no seed scripts, no sync.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: But having GLBs in two locations is untenable. Besides the munchkins, we'll have flying monkeys, flowers — all GLBs, much more.

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: That's exactly why this matters. With per-binding remote flags, you upload a GLB to R2 once, register it in D1 once, and it works everywhere — local dev, production, every developer's machine. The asset library scales to hundreds of models without any local duplication.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: What about the DevalueError I saw in the logs?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: Ah — that's a quirk of remote R2 bindings. When you call `object.writeHttpMetadata(headers)` on a remote R2 object, the metadata contains non-POJO values that miniflare's Devalue serializer can't handle. The fix is simple: wrap it in try/catch. We set Content-Type ourselves based on the file extension anyway, so `writeHttpMetadata` is best-effort, not load-bearing.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: So the architecture is: one R2 bucket, one D1 database, every environment reads from the same source of truth.

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: Exactly. One upload, one registration, works everywhere. The per-binding remote flag is the single line that makes it possible.
